setting.note: Around the endless Chancery suit of Jarndyce and Jarndyce, Dickens draws a whole society, from the fog-bound law courts to the rotting slum of Tom-all-Alone's, from a Lincolnshire great house to the good house in Hertfordshire where Esther Summerson makes a home. The novel maps a real geography: the Court of Chancery and Lincoln's Inn, the slum whose fever spreads to rich and poor alike, and the country estates on which its secrets turn.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Charles Dickens (d. 1870), first published 1852-53. Mapped as a cited work: we store no text and reproduce no passages here. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. This entry reproduces no text; the public-domain original is widely available.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. The original text is in the public domain.
rights.attribution: Bleak House, Charles Dickens (1853). Locations mapped by Read the Places.

palette.note: The fog of the famous opening, lying everywhere over London and thickest of all in the Court of Chancery, the murk in which the great suit devours its victims. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
